L1.area: Key derivation and password hashing configuration

L2.description: A key derivation function uses insecure default configuration values, defaulting to a cryptographically weak hash algorithm and an extremely low iteration count (a single iteration instead of hundreds of thousands), making derived keys vulnerable to brute-force and preimage attacks.

L3.description: The PBKDF2 key derivation implementation defaults to SHA1 as its hash algorithm and uses only a single iteration, rather than a secure hash like SHA256 and a high iteration count (e.g., 250,000+). This makes derived keys extremely vulnerable to offline brute-force attacks.

ground_truth.reason: PBKDF2 defaults to SHA1 and a single iteration, making it 1,000 times weaker than originally specified in 1993 and over 1,300,000 times weaker than current OWASP recommendations, leaving derived keys trivially vulnerable to brute-force attacks.
